Ayahs

وَيْلٌ لِّلْمُطَفِّفِينَ (١)
Woe to the defrauders, who use short measures, (1)
ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ٱكْتَالُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ (٢)
who, when they measure [a commodity bought] from the people, take the full measure, (2)
وَإِذَا كَالُوهُمْ أَو وَّزَنُوهُمْ يُخْسِرُونَ (٣)
but diminish when they measure or weigh for them. (3)
أَلَا يَظُنُّ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَنَّهُم مَّبْعُوثُونَ (٤)
Do they not know that they will be resurrected (4)
لِيَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٥)
on a tremendous day, (5)
يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلنَّاسُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦)
a day when mankind will stand before the Lord of all the worlds? (6)

Content of Surah al-Mutaffifin

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether this Surah was revealed in Makkah or Madinah.

A narration regarding the occasion of revelation indicates that the opening verses of this Surah, which speak about "defrauders" (those who give short measure), refer to individuals in "Madinah" who were engaged in this filthy practice.

However, the tone of the other verses in this Surah bears a strong resemblance to the Makkan Surahs, which announce the Resurrection and the events of the Day of Judgment in short, rhythmic verses. In particular, the verses at the end of this Surah, which describe the disbelievers' mockery of the Muslims, are highly consistent with the environment of Makkah, where the believers were in the minority and the disbelievers were in the overwhelming majority.

Perhaps for this reason, some consider part of the Surah to be "Makkan" and part of it to be "Madinan," but on the whole, this Surah is more similar to the Makkan Surahs.

In any case, the discussions in this Surah revolve around five axes:

  1. A severe warning and threat toward the defrauders.

  2. An indication that great sins stem from a lack of firm faith in the Resurrection.

  3. A portion of the fate of the "wicked" (fujjar) on that Great Day.

  4. A portion of the magnificent rewards and soul-nourishing blessings of the righteous in Paradise.

  5. A reference to the ignorant mockery of the believers by the disbelievers, and the reversal of this situation on the Day of Resurrection.

The Virtue of Reciting this Surah

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

من قرأ سورة "المطففين" سقاه الله من الرحيق المختوم يوم القيامة:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Mutaffifin, Allah will quench his thirst on the Day of Resurrection with a pure, sealed, and exquisite nectar that no hand has ever touched." [1] And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

من قرأ فى فرائضه "ويل للمطففين" اعطاه الامن يوم القيامة من النار، و لم تره، و لم يرها.

"Whoever recites 'Woe to the defrauders' (Waylun lil-Mutaffifin) in his obligatory prayers, Allah will grant him security from the punishment of Hell on the Day of Resurrection; neither shall the Fire see him, nor shall he see the Fire." [2] It is evident that all this reward, virtue, and blessing is for the one who makes its recitation a prelude to action.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 451.

[2]: Thawab al-A'mal, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 527.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ وَيْلٌ لِلْمُطَفِّفِينَ (1) الَّذِينَ إِذَا اكْتالُوا عَلَى النَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ (2) وَ إِذا كالُوهُمْ أَوْ وَزَنُوهُمْ يُخْسِرُونَ (3) أَ لا يَظُنُّ أُولئِكَ أَنَّهُمْ مَبْعُوثُونَ (4) لِيَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (5) يَوْمَ يَقُومُ النَّاسُ لِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ (6)

Occasion of Revelation

Ibn Abbas says: When the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله entered Madinah, many people were heavily involved in the corruption of defrauding (giving short measure). Allah revealed these verses, and they accepted them; thereafter, they abandoned defrauding.

In another hadith, it is mentioned that many of the people of Madinah were merchants and would defraud in their business, and many of their transactions were forbidden (haram). These verses were revealed, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited them to the people of Madinah, then added: "Five for five!" They asked, "O Messenger of Allah, which five are against which five?" He replied:

"No people have ever broken a covenant except that Allah set their enemies over them! And no community has ever judged by other than what Allah revealed except that poverty spread among them! And indecency (fahishah) has never appeared among a nation except that death became frequent among them! And they have never defrauded in measure except that their crops were destroyed and famine overtook them! And no people have ever withheld Zakat except that rain was withheld from them!" [1]

The late Tabarsi also narrated in Majma' al-Bayan regarding the occasion of revelation of these verses that there was a man in Madinah named "Abu Juhaynah" who had two measures, a large one and a small one. When buying, he would use the large measure, and when selling, he would use the small one (this Surah was revealed and warned him and those like him). [2]

Commentary

Woe to the Defrauders!

In these verses, before anything else, He places the defrauders under a severe threat, saying: "Woe to the defrauders!" (وَيْلٌ لِلْمُطَفِّفِينَ).

This is, in fact, a declaration of war from Allah against these oppressive, tyrannical, and filthy individuals who unfairly trample upon the rights of the people.

"Mutaffifin" is derived from the root "tathfif," which originally comes from "taff," meaning the edges or sides of something. The reason the land of "Karbala" is called Wadi al-Taff is that it is situated on the bank of the Euphrates. Subsequently, the word "tafif" was applied to anything small. Similarly, it is applied to a measure that is not full—meaning its contents have reached the edges but have not filled it completely. Later, this term came to be used for defrauding in any shape or form.

"Wayl" here means "evil," "grief and sorrow," "destruction," "painful punishment," or "a harsh and burning valley in Hell." Usually, this word is used when invoking a curse or expressing the ugliness of something, or it is a short expression that evokes many concepts.

It is noteworthy that in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated that Allah has not used "Wayl" for anyone in the Quran unless He has named them a disbeliever, just as He says: فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ مَشْهَدِ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ: "Woe to the disbelievers from the scene of a Great Day." [1] From this narration, it is inferred that defrauding carries the scent of disbelief!

Then, He proceeds to describe the actions of the "mutaffifin" and defrauders, saying: "They are those who, when they take by measure from people, take in full" (الَّذِينَ إِذَا اكْتالُوا عَلَى النَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ). [2]

"But when they give by measure or weight to them, they cause loss" (وَ إِذا كالُوهُمْ أَوْ وَزَنُوهُمْ يُخْسِرُونَ).

A group of commentators has inferred from the above verses that a "mutaffif" is someone who takes more than his right when buying and gives less than the right when selling; therefore, Allah has placed "Wayl" upon them for both reasons.

However, this is a mistake, because the expression "yastawfun" means they take their right in full, and there is nothing in this phrase that indicates taking more than the right. The reason we see Allah censuring them is the comparison of these two states with each other: that when buying, they take the right in full, but when selling, they give it deficiently. It is exactly like when we say in censure of someone: "Whenever he has a debt to collect from someone, he takes it exactly on time, but if he is the debtor, he delays for months"—even though collecting a debt on time is not a bad thing; the bad thing is the comparison between these two behaviors.

It is noteworthy that regarding taking the right, only "measure" (kayl) is mentioned, but regarding paying, both "measure" and "weight" (wazn) are mentioned. This difference in expression may be due to one of the following two reasons:

First, major buyers in the past mostly used "measure," because large scales capable of measuring heavy weights did not exist, but large measuring vessels were easily found (it is also said regarding the term "kurr" that it was originally the name of a large measure). However, when selling, they engaged in both wholesale (by measure) and retail (by weight).

Second, for taking one's right, using a measure is more suitable because cheating in it is more difficult, but for defrauding, using weight is more common because the possibility of cheating in it is greater.

It is also noteworthy that although the above verses only speak of defrauding in terms of measure and weight, the concept of the verse is undoubtedly broad and includes any kind of defrauding, even in items sold by count.

Rather, it is not unlikely, by discarding specificities, that it also encompasses falling short in services. For example, if a worker or an employee steals some of their time, they fall into the category of the "mutaffifin" and defrauders whom the verses of this Surah severely rebuke.

Some wish to grant the verse a broader scope and include any transgression of divine limits and any deficiency in social and moral relations. Although deriving this meaning from the literal words of the verse is not explicit, it is not inconsistent either.

Hence, it is narrated from the famous companion Abdullah ibn Mas'ud that he said: "Prayer is also subject to measure! Whoever fulfills its measure completely, Allah will give him his reward in full, and whoever falls short in it, what Allah said regarding the defrauders (mutaffifin) applies to him." [1]

Then He threatens them with this interrogative sentence of rebuke: "Do they not think that they will be resurrected?" (أَ لا يَظُنُّ أُولئِكَ أَنَّهُمْ مَبْعُوثُونَ).

"On a Great Day" (لِيَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ). A day whose punishment, accounting, news, and terror are all great.

"The Day when mankind will stand before the Lord of the worlds" (يَوْمَ يَقُومُ النَّاسُ لِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

That is, if they believed in the Resurrection and knew that there is an accounting and a book, and that all their deeds are recorded for trial in that great court, and that whoever has done an atom's weight of good or evil will see its result on that Great Day, they would never have committed such oppression and tyranny, nor would they have trampled upon the rights of individuals.

Many commentators consider "yazunnu," which is from the root "zann," to mean "certainty" (yaqin) here, and similar expressions exist in the Holy Quran, such as verse 249 of Surah al-Baqarah: قالَ الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا اللَّهِ كَمْ مِنْ فِئَةٍ قَلِيلَةٍ غَلَبَتْ فِئَةً كَثِيرَةً بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ: "Those who were certain that they would meet Allah (and had faith in the Day of Resurrection) said: 'How many a small company has overcome a large company by Allah's permission!'" (Note that this verse is about a group of the Children of Israel who had shown their faith and steadfastness in various stages).

A clear witness to this is a hadith narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who said in the interpretation of the verse: "Do they not think that they will be resurrected on a Great Day?":

"Its meaning is: Do they not have certainty (yuqinun) that they will be resurrected?" [1] It is also narrated from him عليه السلام that he said: "Opinion (zann) is of two kinds: the opinion of doubt, and the opinion of certainty. Whatever appears in the Quran regarding the Hereafter is the opinion of certainty, and whatever appears regarding the world is the opinion of doubt!" [2]

A group has also suggested the possibility that "zann" here carries its common modern meaning, i.e., "supposition," indicating that mindfulness of the Resurrection affects the human soul and spirit to such an extent that even if someone merely supposes it and considers the possibility of such a day, they should refrain from committing wrongdoings, let alone if they have certainty. This is what is known among scholars as "averting a supposed harm" or "averting a potential harm." The meaning of this statement would be that these reckless and fearless sinners not only lack certainty in the Resurrection but do not even entertain a supposition of it (however, the first interpretation is preferred due to the reasons mentioned).

The important point is that the word "zann," as Raghib says in al-Mufradat, is originally a name for that state which arises in the human mind from the existence of certain indications. If the signs are strong, it brings knowledge and certainty, and if the signs are weak, it does not rise above the level of supposition. [1] Therefore, the aforementioned word, contrary to what is common among us today, has a broad concept that includes both "knowledge" and "supposition," and is used sometimes for this and sometimes for that.

Key Point: Defrauding as a Factor of Corruption on Earth.

In the verses of the Holy Quran, defrauding is repeatedly rebuked. Sometimes in the story of Shu'ayb عليه السلام, where he addresses his people, saying: "Give full measure and do not be of those who cause loss. And weigh with an even balance. And do not deprive people of their due and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption" (Ash-Shu'ara 181-183).

In this way, defrauding and abandoning justice in measure and weight are categorized alongside spreading corruption on earth, and this itself is evidence of the dimensions of the social evils of this act.

Furthermore, in verses 7 and 8 of Surah al-Rahman, observing justice in weight is placed alongside the justice in the system of creation in the universe, where He says: "And the heaven He raised and at the same time He set up the balance, that you may not transgress within the balance." This indicates that the issue of observing justice in measurement is not a small or insignificant matter; rather, it is in fact a part of the principle of justice and the general order governing the entire universe.

For this reason, the great leaders of Islam gave this matter immense importance, to the extent that in the famous narration of Asbagh ibn Nubatah, he says:

I heard Ali عليه السلام saying from atop the pulpit:

"O assembly of merchants! Jurisprudence (fiqh) first, then trade!" And the Imam عليه السلام repeated this statement three times... and at the end of this speech, he said:

"The merchant is a profligate (fajir), and the profligate is in the Fire, except for those who take the right and give the right." [1]

In another hadith narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: "When the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, was in Kufah, he would come every morning to the markets of Kufah and go from market to market, carrying a whip (for punishing violators) on his shoulder. He would stand in the middle of each market and call out: 'O assembly of merchants! Fear Allah!' When they heard the voice of Ali عليه السلام, they would put down whatever was in their hands and listen to his words with all their hearts. Then he would say:

'Seek goodness from Allah, and seek blessing by making things easy for the people, and draw near to the buyers, and adorn yourselves with forbearance, and refrain from swearing, and avoid lying, and shun oppression, and do justice to the oppressed, and do not approach usury, and fulfill the measure and the balance, and do not deprive people of their due, and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption!'"

In this manner, he would circulate through the markets of Kufah, then return to the Government House (Dar al-Imarah) and sit to address the people's grievances. [1]

Also, as mentioned in the occasion of revelation of the verses, the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله says:

"No group ever defrauds except that Allah takes their crops from them and they are afflicted with famine!"

From the sum of what has been said above, it is inferred that one of the important factors in the destruction and punishment of some past nations was the issue of defrauding, which caused the disruption of their economic system and the descent of divine punishment.

Even in Islamic narrations regarding the etiquette of trade, it is mentioned that it is better for believers to give a little more when measuring and weighing, and to take a little less when receiving their right (exactly the opposite of the actions of those mentioned in the above verses who take their right in full and pay the rights of others deficiently). [1] On the other hand, as we indicated in the commentary of the above verse, the issue of defrauding, in the view of some, has a broad meaning that includes any kind of falling short in work and in the fulfillment of individual, social, and divine duties.

[1]: Refer to Vol. 12 of Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Chapters on Trade, Chapter 7, p. 290.

Ayahs

كَلَّآ إِنَّ كِتَـٰبَ ٱلْفُجَّارِ لَفِى سِجِّينٍ (٧)
Indeed, the record of the vicious is in Sijjeen. (7)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ (٨)
And what will show you what is Sijjeen? (8)
كِتَـٰبٌ مَّرْقُومٌ (٩)
It is a written record. (9)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٠)
Woe to the deniers on that day, (10)

Commentary

You Do Not Know What "Sijjin" Is!

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the defrauders and the relationship between sin and the lack of firm belief in the Day of Resurrection, these verses point to a portion of the fate of the wicked and the transgressors on that Day.

First, It says: "It is not as they think about the Resurrection—that there is no accounting or record—rather, the record of the wicked is in Sijjin" (كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتابَ الفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ).

"And what will make you know what Sijjin is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ ما سِجِّينٌ).

"It is a record inscribed" (كِتابٌ مَرْقُومٌ). In the commentary of these verses, there are primarily two theories:

  1. The "Record" (Kitab) refers to the very book of deeds of human beings, in which no action, small or large, minor or major, is omitted; everything is recorded therein without deficiency. And "Sijjin" refers to a comprehensive book in which the records of the deeds of all humans are gathered collectively. To put it simply, it is like a general ledger where the account of each creditor and debtor is recorded on a separate page. However, it is understood from these verses and the subsequent ones that the deeds of all the wicked are gathered in one book called "Sijjin," and the deeds of all the righteous and the virtuous (Abrar) are gathered in another book called "Illiyyin."

"Sijjin" is derived from the root "Sijn," meaning "prison or confinement," and it has various meanings: a harsh and severe prison, any solid and hard object, a terrifying valley at the bottom of Hell, the place where the records of the deeds of the wicked are kept, and the fire of Hell. [1] Turayhi in Majma' al-Bahrayn, under the root "Sijn," says: "It is mentioned in the commentary that it is a comprehensive book, a register of evil other than Allah, in which He has compiled the deeds of the disbelievers and the transgressors among the Jinn and mankind" (Turayhi [1] has not clarified which commentary this refers to, whether it is from an Infallible (Ma'sum) or someone else).

The clues that support this meaning include: 1. The term "Kitab" (Book/Record) in the Holy Quran in such contexts often refers to the record of deeds. 2. The final verse, which is mentioned as an explanation for Sijjin, states that it is an "inscribed record" (Kitabun Marqum). For some to suggest that this verse is not an explanation of Sijjin is certainly contrary to the apparent meaning. 3. Some have said that "Sijjin" and "Sijjil" share the same meaning, and we know that Sijjil (with a kasra on the Sin and Jim and a shadda on the Lam) means a large book. [1] 4. It is understood from other verses of the Quran that human deeds are recorded in several books so that at the time of accounting, no excuse or pretext remains for anyone.

First is the personal record of deeds, which is given to its owners on the Day of Judgment: to the righteous in their right hands and to the wicked in their left hands, as frequently mentioned in Quranic verses. Second is the book that can be called the record of deeds of nations, referred to in Surah Al-Jathiyah, verse 28: "Every nation will be called to its record" (كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى‌ إِلى‌ كِتابِهَا). Third is the general record of all the wicked and the righteous, which is referred to in the verses under discussion and the upcoming verses as "Sijjin" and "Illiyyin."

In summary, according to this commentary, "Sijjin" is that master ledger in which the records of deeds of all the wicked are collected. Referring to it as "Sijjin" may be because the contents of this ledger cause them to be imprisoned in Hell, or the ledger itself is located at the bottom of Hell, unlike the book of the virtuous, which is in the highest "Illiyyin" of Paradise.

  1. The second commentary is that "Sijjin" carries its well-known meaning, which is "Hell," a massive prison for all the wicked, or a particularly harsh place within Hell. The "Record of the Wicked" (Kitab al-Fujjar) refers to the destiny that has been inscribed for them.

Therefore, the meaning of the verse is: "The decreed and certain destiny of the wicked is in Hell." The use of the word "Kitab" (Book/Record) in this sense is not uncommon in the Quran, such as in Surah An-Nisa, verse 24, where after stating that married women are forbidden to you, it adds: "This is the decree of Allah upon you" (كِتابَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ). And in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 75, we read: "And blood relatives are more entitled to each other in the decree of Allah" (وَ أُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى‌ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ).

What supports this commentary is that "Sijjin" has been interpreted as "Hell" in its well-known sense as mentioned in Islamic reports and traditions. In the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is also stated that the meaning of "Indeed, the record of the wicked is in Sijjin" is that what has been decreed for them of punishment is in Sijjin (Hell).

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we also read: "Sijjin is the seventh earth and Illiyyin is the seventh heaven" (referring to the lowest and highest places). [1] It is also understood from several narrations that deeds not worthy of proximity to God fall and are placed in Sijjin, as mentioned in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Indeed, the angel ascends with the deed of a servant joyfully. When he brings up his good deeds, Allah عز و جل says: 'Place it in Sijjin, for he did not intend Me by it!'" [2]

From the collection of these narrations, it appears that "Sijjin" is a very lowly place in Hell where the deeds or the records of the deeds of the wicked are placed, or their destiny is to be trapped in that prison. According to this commentary, the phrase "an inscribed record" (Kitabun Marqum) is an emphasis on the phrase "Indeed, the record of the wicked is in Sijjin" (rather than being a definition for Sijjin), meaning this is a punishment inscribed for them, definitive and certain.

"Marqum" is derived from the root "Raqm," meaning a bold script. Since bold and clear scripts are free of ambiguity, this expression may refer to the certainty and lack of ambiguity of the matter—something that will neither be erased nor forgotten.

There is no obstacle to combining these two commentaries, as Sijjin in the first commentary means the master ledger of the deeds of the wicked, and in the second commentary, it means Hell or the depths of the earth. It is clear that these are cause and effect of one another; that is, when a person's record of deeds is placed in the master ledger of the wicked, that very thing causes him to be dragged to the lowest station and the depths of Hell.

In the final verse under discussion, with a soul-shaking sentence, it refers to the grim end of those who deny the Resurrection, saying: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ). This denial is the source of various sins, including defrauding and injustice. In the first verse, He said: "Woe to the defrauders" (وَيْلٌ لِلْمُطَفِّفِينَ), and here He says: "Woe, that Day, to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ)—an expression that, while concise, refers to various types of painful punishments and terrifying calamities.

It is noteworthy that in the first verse, the talk is of the "defrauders" (Mutaffifin), then of the "wicked" (Fujjar), and in the final verse of the "deniers" (Mukadhdhibin). This clearly shows that there is a close relationship between that belief and those actions, which is reflected more clearly in the upcoming verses.

[^1]: Lisan al-Arab, root "Sijn". [^1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 30, p. 70; and Majma' al-Bahrayn, root "Sajl". [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 530, Hadith 15. [^2]: Ibid, Hadith 19.

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ يُكَذِّبُونَ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (١١)
who deny the Day of Retribution; (11)
وَمَا يُكَذِّبُ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّا كُلُّ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ (١٢)
and none denies it except every sinful transgressor. (12)
إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالَ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣)
When Our signs are recited to him, he says, ‘Myths of the ancients!’ (13)
كَلَّا ۖ بَلْ ۜ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (١٤)
No, that is not the case! Rather, their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning. (14)
كَلَّآ إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّمَحْجُوبُونَ (١٥)
Indeed, they will be alienated from their Lord on that day. (15)
ثُمَّ إِنَّهُمْ لَصَالُوا۟ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٦)
Afterward they will enter hell, (16)
ثُمَّ يُقَالُ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ (١٧)
then told, ‘This is what you used to deny!’ (17)

Commentary

Sins are the Rust of Hearts

In the final verse of the preceding passage, an eloquent reference was made to the ominous fate of the deniers. The verses under discussion first begin by identifying them, stating: "They are those who deny the Day of Recompense" (الَّذِينَ يُكَذِّبُونَ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ).

And then it adds: "And none denies it except every sinful transgressor" (وَ ما يُكَذِّبُ بِهِ إِلَّا كُلُّ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ). This means that the root of denying the Resurrection is not logic, thought, or reasoning; rather, individuals who wish to continuously persist in transgressions and remain immersed in sin deny the Resurrection (note that "athīm" [sinful] is an intensive adjective [Sifah Mushabbahah] and denotes the continuity and persistence of sin).

They wish to continue their wickedness without any sense of responsibility and, in their own imagination, with ultimate freedom and distance from any pressure or discomfort of conscience, recognizing no law. This is similar to what appears in Verse 5 of Surah Al-Qiyamah: بَلْ يُرِيدُ الْإِنْسانُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمامَهُ: "But man desires to continue in sin for the rest of his life; therefore, he denies the Resurrection."

Thus, just as belief affects action, contaminated actions also influence the formation of beliefs. This meaning will become clearer in the commentary of the subsequent verses.

In the next verse, it points to the third characteristic of the deniers of the Resurrection, adding: When Our verses are recited to him, he says: "These are but fables and baseless tales of the ancients" (إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ آياتُنا قالَ أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ). In addition to being transgressors (mu'tad) and sinful (athīm), they also subject the Divine verses to mockery and ridicule, presenting them as a collection of myths, imaginary fables, and worthless words, similar to what has remained as a legacy from the earliest eras (the eras of human ignorance) [1]. By this pretext, they wish to absolve themselves of responsibility toward these verses.

Not only in this instance, but in other verses of the Holy Quran, we read that audacious criminals resorted to this same pretext to escape responding to the Divine call. Indeed, in nine verses of the Holy Quran, this exact matter is reflected—that the polytheists resorted to this foul speech in the face of the verses of the Holy Quran. For example, in Verse 5 of Surah Al-Furqan, we read: وَ قالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ اكْتَتَبَها فَهِيَ تُمْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَ أَصِيلًا: "And they say: 'Fables of the ancients which he has had written down, and they are dictated to him morning and evening!'" In Surah Al-Ahqaf, Verse 17, we read from the tongue of a rebellious youth who stands against his kind and faithful parents, ridiculing all their advice with this sentence, saying: ما هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ: "This is nothing but fables and baseless tales of the ancients."

[^1]: "Asātīr" is the plural of "ustūrah," from the root "satr," and is mostly used for imaginary fables and false words.

Some commentators have said that the verse under discussion was revealed concerning "Nadr bin al-Harith bin Kaladah," the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) cousin, who was among the leaders of disbelief and misguidance. However, it is evident that the revelation of a verse regarding a specific case does not prevent it from being applicable to others as well.

In any case, transgressors always fabricate hollow excuses to escape the reproach of conscience on one hand, and the objections of truth-seeking people on the other, so as to rid themselves of both. It is remarkable that they often had a coordinated and uniform program, as if they were whispering it into each other's ears throughout history—titles such as "sorcery," "soothsaying," "madness," "fables," and the like!

But in the next verse, the Quran once again points to the primary root of their rebellion and defiance, adding: "No! It is not as they imagine; rather, their evil deeds have settled like rust upon their hearts, and they have failed to perceive the truth" (كَلَّا بَلْ رانَ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ). What a jolting expression! Their deeds have poured rust upon their hearts, stripping away the original light and purity that was there by the decree of God-given nature (fitrah). For this reason, the face of truth, which shines like the world-illuminating sun, never finds a reflection therein, and the rays of the lights of revelation are not reflected in it.

"Rāna" is from the root "rayn." As Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: it is the same rust that settles on precious objects. According to other lexicographers, it is the reddish layer that appears on iron and the like due to atmospheric moisture, which we call "rust" (zang or zangār) in Persian. It is usually a sign of the decay and spoilage of that metal, and naturally, the loss of its transparency and brilliance.

Sometimes it has been interpreted as the dominance and mastery of one thing over another, or falling into something from which there is no escape, all of which are actually consequences of the original meaning [1]. Regarding the destructive effect of sin in erasing the luminosity and purity of the heart, we have a discussion that you will read in the "Key Points."

In the next verse, it adds: "No! It is not as they think; rather, they will be veiled from their Lord on that Day" (كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَنْ رَبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَمَحْجُوبُونَ). And this is their most painful punishment, just as the spiritual meeting with the Lord and presence in the court of His proximity is the highest gift and most delightful blessing for the righteous (abrār).

"Kallā" (No!) is usually used to negate a statement previously mentioned, and here commentators have offered several possibilities for it:

First, it is an emphasis on the "Kallā" in the previous verse, meaning: it is not as they claim, that the Day of Resurrection is a fable or myth.

Or, it is not the case that the rust settled on their hearts due to sin will be cleansed; they are deprived of witnessing the Beauty of Truth both in this world and in the next.

Or, as mentioned in other verses of the Quran, they claimed that...

[^1]: Refer to the commentary of Fakhr al-Razi under the verse and Al-Munjid under the root "rayn."

...even if there were a Resurrection, they would be recipients of various blessings from God there [1]! It is not as they imagine; rather, in the Resurrection, they will be in the severest of punishments and the harshest of tortures.

Indeed, the Hereafter is a reflection and a grand manifestation of human deeds in this world. Those who close their eyes here to the observation of Truth, and whose deeds have settled like rust upon their hearts, will also be veiled from their Lord there, and will never have the power to witness the Beauty of Truth in that world, being deprived of the grace of meeting that True Beloved.

"Then they shall surely enter the Hellfire and remain there" (ثُمَّ إِنَّهُمْ لَصالُوا الْجَحِيمِ). This entry into the Fire is the result of being veiled from the Lord; it is an effect inseparable from it. Surely, the fire of deprivation from seeing the Truth is even more burning than the fire of Hell!

And in the final verse, He says: "Then it will be said to them: 'This is what you used to deny!'" (ثُمَّ يُقالُ هذَا الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ).

[^1]: In Verse 32 of Surah Al-Kahf, it says: وَ ما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَ لَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ إِلى‌ رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْراً مِنْها مُنْقَلَباً: "I do not think the Hour will ever come, and even if I am returned to my Lord, I will surely find a better place of return than this" (a similar meaning also appears in Surah Fussilat, Verse 50).

This statement is said to them by way of rebuke, reproach, and blame; it is a spiritual punishment and a psychological torture for this headstrong and stubborn group.

Key Points

1- Why is Sin the Rust of the Heart?

Not only in the verses of this Surah is the effect of sin in darkening the heart mentioned, but in many other verses of the Holy Quran, this meaning is repeatedly and explicitly addressed.

In one place, He says: كَذلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلُّ قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ جَبَّارٍ: "Thus does Allah set a seal over every heart of a proud, rebellious one" (Ghafir: 35).

And in another place, regarding a group of stubborn and antagonistic sinners, He says: خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَ عَلى‌ سَمْعِهِمْ وَ عَلى‌ أَبْصارِهِمْ غِشاوَةٌ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ: "Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and upon their hearing, and over their vision is a veil, and for them is a great punishment" (Al-Baqarah: 7).

And in Verse 46 of Surah Al-Hajj, we read: فَإِنَّها لا تَعْمَى الْأَبْصارُ وَ لكِنْ تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ: "For indeed, it is not the eyes that are blinded, but blinded are the hearts which are within the breasts."

Indeed, the worst effect of sin and its continuation is the darkening of the heart and the destruction of the light of knowledge and the sense of discernment. Sins flow from the limbs and organs into the heart, turning the heart into a stagnant, foul-smelling swamp. It is here that man fails to distinguish the path from the pit and commits strange errors that bewilder everyone. With his own hand, he strikes at the root of his own prosperity and squanders the capital of his happiness.

In a Hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "Abundance of sins corrupts the heart" [1]. And in another Hadith from him, we read: "When a servant commits a sin, a black spot appears in his heart. If he repents, desists, and seeks forgiveness, his heart is polished. But if he returns to sin, the blackness increases until it overcomes his heart. That is the rust (rayn) which Allah mentioned in the Quran: 'No! Rather, their deeds have rusted their hearts'" [2]. This same meaning is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) in Usul al-Kafi with a slight variation [3]. Also, in the same book, it is narrated from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) that he said: "Discuss with one another, meet one another, and narrate Hadith, for Hadith is the polish of the hearts. Indeed, hearts rust just as a sword rusts, and its polish is Hadith" [4].

[^1]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 326. [^2]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 325. [^3] & [^4]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 531, Hadith 22 & 23.

From the perspective of psychological principles, it is also proven that human actions always have a reflection in the soul, gradually shaping the soul into their own likeness; they even influence one's thinking, reflection, and judgment.

It is also noteworthy that by continuing in sin, man sinks moment by moment into greater spiritual darkness, reaching a point where his sins appear as virtues to him, and he sometimes takes pride in his sin! At this stage, the paths of return are closed to him, and all bridges behind him are destroyed. This is the most dangerous state that can occur for a human being.

2- The Veil over the Soul's Face!

Although many commentators have tried to assume something omitted in the verse "No! Indeed, they will be veiled from their Lord on that Day," saying: these sinners are veiled from God's "mercy," or from His "kindness," "generosity," and "reward."

However, it seems the verse needs no such assumption. They are truly veiled from the Lord. While the good and pure find their way to the proximity of His presence and benefit from the pleasure of meeting the Beloved and His internal witnessing—which is the highest possible pleasure—these faithless and sinful dwellers of Hell are deprived of this great grace and peerless blessing.

Some pure-hearted believers are blessed with the grace of meeting Him even in this world, while the blind-hearted criminals are deprived of this grace even here. The former are constantly in His presence, while these dark-hearted ones, lacking vision, are far away!

The former derive such pleasure from intimate conversation (munajat) with Him that it cannot be described by any words, while the latter are so drowned in the wretchedness of their sins that they have no way of salvation. As the poet says: "You who do not step out from the house of nature, How can you pass through the alley of Truth?! The Beauty of the Friend has no veil or curtain, But settle the dust of the road so that you may behold!"

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), says in the famous Supplication of Kumayl: "Suppose I could endure Your punishment, how could I endure separation from You?!"

Ayahs

كَلَّآ إِنَّ كِتَـٰبَ ٱلْأَبْرَارِ لَفِى عِلِّيِّينَ (١٨)
Indeed, the record of the pious is in Illeeyun. (18)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ (١٩)
And what will show you what is Illeeyun? (19)
كِتَـٰبٌ مَّرْقُومٌ (٢٠)
It is a written record, (20)
يَشْهَدُهُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ (٢١)
witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]. (21)
إِنَّ ٱلْأَبْرَارَ لَفِى نَعِيمٍ (٢٢)
The pious shall be amid bliss, (22)
عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ يَنظُرُونَ (٢٣)
observing, [as they recline] on couches. (23)
تَعْرِفُ فِى وُجُوهِهِمْ نَضْرَةَ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٢٤)
You will perceive in their faces the freshness of bliss. (24)
يُسْقَوْنَ مِن رَّحِيقٍ مَّخْتُومٍ (٢٥)
They will be served with a sealed pure wine, (25)
خِتَـٰمُهُۥ مِسْكٌ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَتَنَافَسِ ٱلْمُتَنَـٰفِسُونَ (٢٦)
whose seal is musk—for such let the viers vie— (26)
وَمِزَاجُهُۥ مِن تَسْنِيمٍ (٢٧)
and whose seasoning is from Tasneem, (27)
عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ (٢٨)
a spring where those brought near [to Allah] drink. (28)

Commentary

'Illiyyun Awaits the Righteous

Following the description in the previous verses regarding the "Fajjar" (the wicked), their record of deeds, and their destiny, these verses speak of the opposite group—the "Abrar" (the righteous and the virtuous). Observing their honors and privileges in contrast to the wicked makes the position of both groups clearer.

First, It says: "Nay! Truly, the record of the righteous is in 'Illiyyun" (كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتابَ الْأَبْرارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ).

"'Illiyyun" is the plural of "'Aliyy" (on the weight of maliyy). Originally, it means a high place or persons who sit in a high position; it is also applied to the inhabitants of the elevated parts of mountains. Here, some have interpreted it as "the highest place in heaven" or "the highest station in Paradise."

Some have also said that its mention in the plural form is for emphasis, meaning 'uluwwun fi 'uluww (height upon height).

In any case, two interpretations similar to those we had for "Sijjin" in the previous verses are applicable here as well:

First, that "the record of the righteous" refers to the book of deeds of the virtuous, the pure, and the believers. The aim is to state that their record of deeds is placed in a master register that accounts for all the deeds of the believers—a register that is highly exalted and of a lofty status.

Alternatively, it means their record of deeds is placed above them in the most honorable place, or above Paradise in the highest station. All of this indicates that their own status is exceptionally high and sublime.

In a Hadith, we read that the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "By 'Illiyyun is meant the seventh heaven under the Throne of Allah." [^1] This is exactly the opposite of the record of the "Fajjar," which is placed in the lowest place or the bottom layers of Hell.

Another interpretation is that "Kitab" (Record/Book) here means the destiny and the definitive Divine decree which has ordained that the righteous shall be in the highest degrees of Paradise.

Of course, it is possible to combine these two interpretations: that their record of deeds is in a master register, that the entire register is above the heavens, and that the Divine command is set upon it that they themselves shall be in the highest degrees of Paradise.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 7053; Majma' al-Bahrayn, under the root "‘Alw".

Then, to express the importance and greatness of "'Illiyyun," He adds: "And what will explain to you what 'Illiyyun is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ ما عِلِّيُّونَ). This indicates that it is a status and a place beyond "imagination, comparison, conjecture, and fancy," such that no one—not even the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—can fully grasp the dimensions of its greatness.

Then the Quran itself provides further explanation, adding: "[It is] a written record" (كِتابٌ مَرْقُومٌ). This is based on the interpretation that identifies "'Illiyyun" as the master register of the deeds of the righteous. However, according to the other interpretation, the meaning of the verse is: "This is a certain destiny that Allah has inscribed for them, that their place shall be the highest degrees of Paradise." (Therefore, "Kitabun Marqum" is an explanation for "Kitab al-Abrar," not for "'Illiyyun"—take note).

Then He adds: "Witnessed by those nearest [to Allah]" (يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ). Although a group of commentators has interpreted "al-Muqarrabun" in this verse as the angels who are near the Divine Presence—angels who oversee the record of deeds or the certain destiny of the righteous—the subsequent verses clearly show that "al-Muqarrabun" are a group of the elite and chosen ones among the believers who hold a very high rank and are witnesses to the record of deeds of other righteous people. This is similar to verses 10 and 11 of Surah al-Waqi'ah, where after mentioning the two groups "the People of the Right" and "the People of the Left," He says: وَ السَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ ("And the foremost are the foremost; those are the ones brought near").

And in Surah an-Nahl, verse 89, we read: وَ يَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ فِي كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَ جِئْنا بِكَ شَهِيداً عَلى‌ هؤُلاءِ ("And [mention] the Day when We will resurrect among every nation a witness over them from themselves, and We will bring you [O Muhammad] as a witness over these").

Then, He begins to describe part of the great rewards of the righteous, saying: "Indeed, the righteous will be in pleasure" (إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ لَفِي نَعِيمٍ).

The primary concept of "Na'im," which according to Raghib means "abundant blessing," combined with its mention in an indefinite form (which here signifies greatness and importance), shows that they possess such blessings and bounties that cannot be described. This is a comprehensive and concise expression referring to all the material and spiritual gifts of Paradise.

Then He describes some of them, saying: "On adorned couches, observing" (عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ يَنْظُرُونَ). [^1] They lean on beautiful heavenly thrones and look at those beautiful scenes and all those blessings, finding delight in them.

[^1]: In this sentence, the "subject" (mubtada) is omitted and is implied as "Hum 'ala al-ara'iki yanzurun" (They are on couches, observing), where "yanzurun" is a circumstantial clause (hal); or "‘ala al-ara’ik" is a second predicate for "Inna" in the previous verse.

"Ara'ik" is the plural of "arikah," meaning beautiful royal thrones, or highly decorated couches placed in a "bridal chamber." Here, it refers to the exceptionally beautiful heavenly thrones upon which the righteous lean.

Some believe the origin of this word is Persian, derived from "Arg," meaning a royal palace (this term also refers to a citadel within a city, and since citadels were often reserved for kings, it was applied to them).

Others consider "Ara'ik" to be singular and derived from the Persian word "Arak" or "Ara'ik," meaning a king's throne; it was later applied to the "capital" or the province where the capital is located. They consider "Iraq" to be the Arabized form of "Arak," meaning such a province, and say the word "Ara'ik" meaning a court or royal throne also appears in the Avesta.

While some Arabic linguists consider its root to be Arabic, believing it is derived from the word "Arak," which is the name of a well-known tree from which thrones and canopies were made. [^1] However, what is understood from the usage of this word in the Holy Quran is that it means those beautiful and decorated thrones used by the possessors of blessing and power.

In the expression "yanzurun" (they observe/look), which is stated concisely, He does not specify what they are looking at, so that it may have a broad and expansive meaning. Yes, they look at the grace of Allah, at His peerless Beauty, at the various heavenly blessings, and at the dazzling beauties found in the highest Paradise; for one of the most important human pleasures is the "pleasure of sight and looking."

[^1]: Refer to Dehkhoda Dictionary, the book Diwan-e Din, Mufradat al-Raghib, and Burhan-e Qati'.

Then He adds: "You will recognize in their faces the radiance of delight" (تَعْرِفُ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ نَضْرَةَ النَّعِيمِ). This indicates that vigor, joy, and happiness surge in their faces, and there is no need to ask. In contrast, if you look at the faces of the people of Hell, grief, pain, sorrow, misery, and helplessness are evident.

"Nadhrah," as we have mentioned before, refers to the freshness and vigor apparent in the faces of those living a life of luxury.

After the blessings of "thrones," "sight," and "tranquility and vigor," He mentions another blessing—the drink of the people of Paradise—adding: "They will be given to drink of a pure wine, sealed" (يُسْقَوْنَ مِنْ رَحِيقٍ مَخْتُومٍ). A pure drink that, unlike the contaminated and satanic wine of this world, does not cause sin or madness, but rather creates intelligence, reason, joy, love, and purity.

Most commentators have taken "Rahiq" to mean pure wine—a wine that has no impurity, adulteration, or contamination. The description "Makhtum" (sealed), which in modern Persian is replaced by "dar-basteh" (closed) or "polomp" (sealed), is also an emphasis on its purity, cleanliness, and untouched nature.

Furthermore, the use of such vessels is a sign of special respect for the guest—a vessel that has been closed and sealed, and its seal is broken only by the guest! [^1]

Then He says: "The seal thereof is musk" (خِتامُهُ مِسْكٌ). Not like the sealed vessels of this world whose seal is made of "clay," and when a person wants to open a sealed object by breaking its seal, his hand becomes soiled. The pure heavenly drink is not like that; when they place their hand on its seal, the fragrance of musk spreads through the air!

Some have also said it means that at the "khitam" (conclusion/end) of drinking this pure wine, the mouth smells of musk—unlike the contaminated wines of the world, after which the mouth becomes bitter and foul-smelling. However, given the expression in the previous verse, this interpretation seems unlikely.

At the end of the verse, after mentioning the qualities of the pure heavenly wine, He says: "And for this [blessing] let the competitors compete" (وَ فِي ذلِكَ فَلْيَتَنافَسِ الْمُتَنافِسُونَ).

The great commentator Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: "Tanafus" means the desire (and effort) of two people where each wants a precious thing possessed by the other to be available to him as well.

[^1]: In the past and present, it has been customary that to ensure something is untouched, it is placed in a vessel, and after closing the lid, it is tied with a rope or wire and knotted. On the knot, some stiff clay or melted lead is placed and then stamped with a seal, such that access to the inside is impossible without breaking that seal. The Arabs call this "Makhtum."

In Majma' al-Bahrayn, it is stated that "Tanafus" means racing and noble outdoing of another (healthy competition and rivalry).

Raghib in al-Mufradat also says: "Munafasah" means a person's effort to resemble distinguished individuals and join them, without causing harm to another.

In reality, the content of the verse is similar to what appears in Surah al-Hadid, verse 21: سابِقُوا إِلى‌ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَ جَنَّةٍ عَرْضُها كَعَرْضِ السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ ("Race toward forgiveness from your Lord and a Garden whose width is like the width of the heavens and earth").

Or what appears in Surah Al-i-Imran, verse 133: وَ سارِعُوا إِلى‌ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَ جَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا السَّماواتُ وَ الْأَرْضُ.

In any case, the expression used in the verse is the most beautiful expression imaginable for encouraging humans to attain these peerless blessings through faith and righteous deeds, reflecting the eloquence of the Holy Quran in the most sublime manner. [^1] [^2]

[^1]: From what has been said in the commentary of the verse, it becomes clear that the referent of "that" (dhalika) is all the heavenly blessings, especially the unique pure wine whose interesting qualities are mentioned in the verse.

[^2]: Since the "waw" and "fa" in the phrase وَ فِي ذلِكَ فَلْيَتَنافَسِ الْمُتَنافِسُونَ are both conjunctions, the question arises as to how two conjunctions come one after another. The most appropriate answer is that a conditional particle is omitted here, and the implied meaning is: "And if competition is desired in anything, then let the competitors compete in this." Thus, the conditional particle and the conditional clause are omitted, and "fi dhalika" is placed forward for emphasis.

Then He mentions the last blessing in this series of verses, saying: "And its mixture is of Tasnim" (وَ مِزاجُهُ مِنْ تَسْنِيمٍ).

"A spring from which those nearest [to Allah] drink" (عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ). [^1] It is understood from these verses that "Tasnim" is the most superior pure heavenly wine, which the "Muqarrabun" (those brought near) drink in its pure form, but for the "Abrar" (the righteous), a portion of it is mixed with "Rahiq Makhtum," which is another type of pure heavenly wine.

As for why this wine or this spring is called "Tasnim" (considering that "Tasnim" linguistically means a spring that pours down from above), some have said it is because this specific spring is located in the upper levels of Paradise, and others have said it is a wine that descends from the sky of Paradise!

In reality, there are various types of heavenly wines: some flow in rivers, as mentioned in several verses of the Quran; [^2] some are in sealed, marked vessels, as mentioned in the verses above; and most importantly, there is the wine that descends from the sky of Paradise or its upper levels, and this is the "Tasnim" wine, which none of the heavenly drinks can equal. Naturally, the effect it has on the soul and spirit of the people of Paradise is the most sublime, most attractive, and deepest, and the spiritual intoxication resulting from it is indescribable.

Of course, we must repeat this truth once more: all of these are but shadows seen from afar; otherwise, the description of the precious and peerless blessings of Paradise is not possible with any tongue or pen, and as the Quran itself says, it does not even cross anyone's mind: فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ ("And no soul knows what has been hidden for them of comfort for eyes" - Surah as-Sajdah, 17).

[^1]: Regarding why "‘aynan" is in the accusative case (mansub), many reasons have been given, including: that it is a circumstantial clause (hal) for Tasnim, or a specification (tamyiz), or for praise and specialization (implied "a'ni" - I mean), and the "ba" in "biha" is either extra or means "min" (from), and the second meaning is more appropriate.

[^2]: Surah Muhammad, verse 15.

Key Points

1- Who are the "Abrar" and the "Muqarrabun"?

In the verses of the Holy Quran, mention is repeatedly made of the "Abrar" (the righteous) and the "Muqarrabun" (those brought near) and their great statuses and rewards, to the extent that according to Surah Al-i-Imran, verse 193, the "Ulul-Albab" (people of profound intellect) request that the end of their lives be with the righteous: وَ تَوَفَّنا مَعَ الْأَبْرارِ ("and take our souls with the righteous").

In the verses of Surah ad-Dahr (al-Insan), very significant rewards are mentioned for them (verses 5 to 22).

In Surah al-Infitar, verse 13, and the verses under discussion (Surah al-Mutaffifin), Divine favors toward them are repeatedly mentioned.

The "Abrar," as we have said before, is the plural of "Barr"—those who have an expansive soul, high ambition, and good belief and action. The "Muqarrabun" possess proximity of status in the presence of Allah. Apparently, the relationship between these two is "general and specific in an absolute sense" ('umum wa khusus mutlaq); that is, all the Muqarrabun are among the Abrar, but not all the Abrar are in the rank of the Muqarrabun.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Mujtaba عليه السلام, we read:

"Wherever in the Book of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, it says 'Inna al-Abrar' (Indeed the righteous...), by Allah, He intended by it no one except Ali ibn Abi Talib, Fatimah, myself, and al-Husayn عليه السلام." [^1]

Undoubtedly, the Khamsah al-Tayyibah (the Five Pure Ones), those five sacred lights, are the clearest examples of the Abrar and Muqarrabun. As we said in the commentary of Surah ad-Dahr, that Surah primarily refers to the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn, and eighteen of its verses discuss their virtues, although the revelation of the verses regarding them does not prevent the generality and expansion of the verses' meaning.

2- Heavenly Drinks!

From various verses of the Quran, it is well understood that in Paradise there are various types of pure wines with diverse names and qualities, which differ in every respect from the contaminated wines of this world. The latter take away the intellect, create madness, and are the source of enmity, bloodshed, and corruption; they are foul-smelling, bad-tasting, and contaminated. But the former create intellect, joy, love, and purity; they are fragrant, perfumed, and pure. Those who drink from them fall into a spiritual and indescribable intoxication. Two types are mentioned in this Surah: "Rahiq Makhtum" and "Tasnim," and other types are mentioned in Surah ad-Dahr and other Surahs of the Quran, each of which we have explained in its place.

Interestingly, in several narrations, this heavenly wine is mentioned as a reward for those who abstain from the contaminated wine of this world, quench the thirst of the thirsty, and extinguish the fire of grief in the hearts of the believers.

The Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام:

"O Ali, whoever leaves wine for the sake of Allah, Allah will give him to drink of the Rahiq Makhtum." [^1]

And even more interestingly, in another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated that even if one leaves the wine of the world for other than Allah, Allah will give him to drink of this pure wine. Ali عليه السلام says: "I asked: 'For other than Allah?' He replied: 'Yes! Whoever abstains from the wine of the world even to preserve his own life, Allah will give him to drink of the Rahiq Makhtum.'" [^2] Indeed, this group who leave wine to preserve their health are in reality among the "Ulul-Albab" (people of intellect), and as understood from Al-i-Imran verse 193, the "Ulul-Albab" are also among the Abrar who benefit from the pure heavenly wines.

In a Hadith from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, it is said:

"Whoever gives a thirsty believer a drink, Allah will give him to drink of the Rahiq Makhtum." [^3]

And in another Hadith:

"Whoever fasts for Allah on a summer day, Allah will quench his thirst on the Day of Resurrection with Rahiq Makhtum." [^4]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 533 (Hadith 33). [^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 534 (Hadith 40 and 37). [^3]: Ibid, Hadith 35. [^4]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed (Vol. 10, p. 456).

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ كَانُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يَضْحَكُونَ (٢٩)
Indeed the guilty used to laugh at the faithful, (29)
وَإِذَا مَرُّوا۟ بِهِمْ يَتَغَامَزُونَ (٣٠)
and when they passed them by they would wink at each other, (30)
وَإِذَا ٱنقَلَبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمُ ٱنقَلَبُوا۟ فَكِهِينَ (٣١)
and when they returned to their folks they would return amused, (31)
وَإِذَا رَأَوْهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَضَآلُّونَ (٣٢)
and when they saw them they would say, ‘Indeed those are the astray!’ (32)
وَمَآ أُرْسِلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ حَـٰفِظِينَ (٣٣)
Though they were not sent to watch over them. (33)
فَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْكُفَّارِ يَضْحَكُونَ (٣٤)
So today the faithful will laugh at the faithless, (34)
عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ يَنظُرُونَ (٣٥)
observing from their couches: (35)
هَلْ ثُوِّبَ ٱلْكُفَّارُ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٣٦)
Have the faithless been requited for what they used to do? (36)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have cited two occasions of revelation for these verses:

First, one day Ali عليه السلام and a group of believers passed by a gathering of the Meccan disbelievers; they laughed at Ali عليه السلام and the believers and mocked them. These verses were revealed, clarifying the fate of this mocking group of disbelievers on the Day of Resurrection.

Hakim Abu al-Qasim Haskani in "Shawahid al-Tanzil" narrates from Ibn Abbas: "The meaning of 'إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا' (Indeed, those who committed crimes) refers to the hypocrites of the Quraysh, and 'الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا' (those who believed) refers to Ali bin Abi Talib and his companions." [^1] Second, these verses were revealed regarding individuals such as Ammar, Suhayb, Khabbab, Bilal, and other poor believers who were subjected to the mockery of the Quraysh polytheists like Abu Jahl, Walid bin Mughirah, and As bin Wa'il. [^2] Reconciling these two occasions of revelation is also entirely possible.

Commentary

That day they mocked the believers, but today...

Following the previous verses which spoke of the immense blessings and rewards of the Abrar (the righteous), the verses under discussion point to a portion of the hardships and troubles they face in this world for the sake of faith and piety, so it becomes clear that those great rewards are not without reason.

In these verses, it speaks of the ugly and offensive stance and conduct of the disbelievers toward them, recounting four types of reactions from the disbelievers:

First, He says: "Indeed, those who committed crimes used to laugh at those who believed" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا كانُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَضْحَكُونَ).

A mocking and belittling laugh; a laugh arising from a spirit of rebellion, arrogance, pride, and negligence. Frivolous and arrogant individuals have always had such drunken laughs in the face of pious believers.

Furthermore, the use of the term "أجرموا" (committed crimes) instead of "كفروا" (disbelieved) shows that disbelieving and faithless individuals can be recognized by their sinful deeds, as disbelief is always the source of crime and disobedience.

In the next verse, it describes their second ugly encounter, saying: "And when they passed by them, they would wink at one another [in mockery]" (وَ إِذا مَرُّوا بِهِمْ يَتَغامَزُونَ). With these signs and gestures, they would say: "Look at these nobodies who have become the close ones to God's threshold! Look at these ragged and barefoot people who claim divine revelation descends upon them! And look at this ignorant group who say decayed bones and dust will return to life once more?!" and similar ugly and hollow statements.

It appears that the overt laughter of the polytheists occurred when the believers passed by their gathering, and their mocking gestures occurred when, conversely, they passed by the gathering of the believers. Since they could not easily mock within that gathering, they would communicate their points to one another through winks and gestures. However, where the polytheists were gathered and the Muslims passed by them, they had more freedom and audacity. [^1] Yataghamazun is derived from the root ghamz, meaning to signal with the eye or hand toward something being criticized. Sometimes this word is used for any kind of fault-finding, even if done with the tongue. The form taghamuz (from the tafa'ul pattern) indicates that they all participated in this act together, each signaling to the other and saying something mocking.

These were all in their encounters with the believers; in their private sessions, they would recount the same program and continue the mockery in their absence, as the next verse says: "And when they returned to their people, they would return jesting" (وَ إِذَا انْقَلَبُوا إِلى‌ أَهْلِهِمُ انْقَلَبُوا فَكِهِينَ). As if they had achieved a victory they were proud of, and again in absence, the same ridicule and mockery continued.

[^1]: Regarding the referent of the pronoun in "مروا" (they passed) and the pronoun "بهم" (by them), commentators have offered two possibilities: some refer the first to the polytheists and the second to the believers, while others say the opposite. However, according to what we mentioned above, the first possibility is more appropriate.

Fakihin is the plural of fakih, an adjective derived from fakahah, meaning to joke and laugh. Originally, it is taken from fakihah meaning fruit; as if these conversations and jokes are like fruits they enjoy. Sweet and friendly conversation is called fukahah.

Although the word ahl usually means family and close relatives, it may have a broader meaning here, including close friends as well.

The fourth malicious reaction against the believers was that "when they saw them, they said, 'Indeed, these are truly lost'" (وَ إِذا رَأَوْهُمْ قالُوا إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ لَضالُّونَ). Because they had abandoned the way of idol-worship and the superstitions prevalent among them—which they considered guidance—and had turned toward faith in Allah and pure monotheism, and in their estimation, had sold the immediate pleasure of this world for the "credit" blessings of the Hereafter.

It is possible that this expression occurred at stages where the matter had moved beyond mockery, and they felt compelled to show more severity. For whenever great Prophets and new religions appeared, the reaction of enemies and opponents initially manifested through mockery and not taking it seriously, as if they viewed the new religion as too insignificant to deal with seriously. But when the divine religion began to penetrate the hearts of ready individuals and gained more followers, they felt threatened, took it seriously, and intensified the struggle.

Then, step by step, they escalated it, and the above verse is the first stage of their serious encounter, which in later stages even led to bloody wars and armed conflict.

Since the believers were often individuals who did not possess significant social status or wealth, and for this reason, the disbelievers looked upon them with contempt, considered their faith worthless, and mocked their religion, the Quran says in the next verse: "But they had not been sent as guardians over them" (وَ ما أُرْسِلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ حافِظِينَ). So by what right and according to what logic do they criticize them?! In verse 27 of Surah Hud, we read that the wealthy and arrogant of Noah's people said to him: "And we do not see you followed except by those who are the lowest of us [and] immature in opinion."

And he replied: "And I do not say to those whom your eyes despise that Allah will never grant them any good. Allah is most knowing of what is within their souls" (Hud: 31). This is, in fact, an answer to these selfish and pretentious individuals: what business is it of yours which group the believers belong to?! You should look at the essence of the call and the content of the religion of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

But in the Hereafter, the matter is reversed, as the next verse says:

"So today those who believed are laughing at the disbelievers" (فَالْيَوْمَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنَ الْكُفَّارِ يَضْحَكُونَ).

For the Resurrection is a reflection of human deeds in this world, and there, divine justice must be implemented. Justice requires that there, the pure-hearted believers laugh at the stubborn, antagonistic, and mocking disbelievers, and this itself is a kind of painful punishment for these arrogant oppressors! In some narrations from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "On that day, a door of Paradise will be opened to the disbelievers, and they, thinking that the command for release from Hell and entry into Paradise has been given, move toward it. When they reach it, suddenly the door is closed. This happens several times, and the believers, watching them from Paradise, laugh!" [^1]

Therefore, in the next verse, it adds: "On adorned couches, observing" (عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ يَنْظُرُونَ). What are they observing? All those endless divine blessings, those great favors and boundless graces, that peace, greatness, and respect, and those painful punishments that the arrogant and selfish disbelievers are afflicted with in the ultimate humiliation and abasement.

Finally, in the last verse of this Surah, it says in the form of an interrogative sentence: "Have the disbelievers been rewarded for what they used to do?"

[^1]: "Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 328 (with slight variation).

(هَلْ ثُوِّبَ الْكُفَّارُ ما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ) [^1] Whether this statement is from Allah, the angels, or the believers, it is a kind of taunt and mockery toward the thoughts and claims of these arrogant oppressors who expected to receive a prize or reward from Allah for their ugly deeds. In response to this false notion and raw fantasy, He says: "Have they received the reward for their deeds?"

Many commentators have considered this sentence an "independent sentence," while some believe it is a continuation of the previous verse, meaning: the believers are sitting on adorned couches and looking to see if the disbelievers have received the reward for their contrary deeds. Yes, if they are to receive a reward, they must receive it from Satan; can that afflicted wretch give them any reward?! Thawwab is from the root thawb, originally meaning the return of something to its primary state. Thawab is the reward given to a person for their deeds because the result of their deeds returns to them. This word is used for both good and bad recompense, although it is mostly used for good. [^2] Therefore, the above verse indicates a kind of taunt toward the disbelievers, and it must be so, for they always mocked the believers and the signs of Allah, and on that day, they must see the result of their mockeries.

[^1]: The interrogation in this verse is a "rhetorical question" (affirmative). [^2]: "Mufradat" of Raghib, root thawb.

Key Point: Mockery, the Unfair Weapon of the Enemies of Truth

Throughout the history of the Prophets, we repeatedly see that one of the weapons used by the enemies of God against them was the weapon of ridicule. Quranic verses have reflected this subject many times. Since ridicule and mockery usually stem from those who are intoxicated with the wine of pride, consider themselves superior to others, and look at others with contempt, it is not surprising that the oppressive, stubborn, and selfish disbelievers would resort to such a weapon against the people of faith.

Today, the same program continues in various forms in the world's mass media under the titles of "satire," comedy, and entertainment programs. Even now, they strive to push the truth and its supporters out of the field using this ancient weapon.

But the believers find peace in the face of them, considering the divine promises—an example of which appears in the verses above—and the spirit of resistance against those dark-hearted ones is breathed into them.

Fundamentally, "mockery," "ridicule," "winking," and "laughing" at the truth, as mentioned in the above verses, are all major sins and signs of ignorance and pride. A wise, rational, and alert person, even assuming they do not believe in a school of thought, deals with it logically and never allows themselves to resort to such weapons.

O Allah! Protect us all from pride, ignorance, and arrogance. O Lord! Grant us the spirit of truth-seeking and humility. O God! Place the record of our deeds in "Illiyyin" and take us out from the ranks of the "Sijjiniyan."

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Mutaffifin.
